message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>OBJECTIVE</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To study the effect of weather on severity indicators of coronavirus disease 2019 (covid-19).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>DESIGN</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Ecological study.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>SETTING</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Paris region.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>POPULATION</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Severely ill patients with covid-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>MAIN OUTCOME MEASURES</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Daily covid-19-related intensive care unit (ICU) admission and in-hospital deaths in the Paris region, and the daily weather characteristics of Paris midtown.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>RESULTS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Daily ICU admissions and in-hospital deaths were strongly and negatively correlated to ambient temperatures, with a time lag. The highest Pearson correlation coefficients and statistically significant P values were found 8 days before occurrence of ICU admissions and 15 days before deaths.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>CONCLUSIONS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The study findings show a strong effect of previously observed ambient temperature that has an effect on severity indicators of covid-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Strengths and limitations of this study</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We assessed the link between weather characteristics with a time lag and covid-19-related hospital severity indicators.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>This is the first study, with reliable data, to find a strong negative correlation between previously observed ambient temperature with covid-19-related iCU admissions and in-hospital deaths.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>It is compatible with the natural history (including hospitalisation) of covid-19 outbreak in a highly developed country.</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>We also report the duration after which iCU admissions and in-Hospital deaths occurs following temperature variation (respectively median of 8 and 15 days).</jats:p>
                  <jats:p>Our analyses are mainly valid for temperate climate countries.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: ICU admissions and in-hospital deaths linked to covid-19 in the Paris region are correlated with previously observed ambient temperature
